one_line_takeaway: HogVul couples lexical (PSO-driven variable renaming) and syntax (control-flow rewriting) code perturbations in one stagnation-aware, cooperative PSO search, beating single-channel black-box baselines ALERT and DIP by an average of ~26 ASR points across three vulnerability-detection benchmarks and three CodeBERT-family target models, while also transferring well to an unseen D2A distribution-shift test.
key_finding: Averaged across the main three datasets and three target models, HogVul improves ASR by 26.37 percentage points over ALERT (lexical-only baseline) and 25.72 points over DIP (syntax-only, dead-code-insertion baseline), for a stated overall average improvement of 26.05% cited in the abstract/intro; the paper's own Table 1 per-cell 'Our Improvement%' column is itself the average of the gap to ALERT and the gap to DIP for that cell (e.g. on Devign/CodeT5: (97.28-81.51 + 97.28-53.05)/2 = 30.0, matching the reported +30.01). Best single ASR result is 97.28% (CodeT5 on Devign), which also matches the full-component (C7) ablation configuration. HogVul also induces larger average confidence drop (Delta_drop) than both baselines in nearly all cells, and higher CAD (population diversity) throughout, though DIP retains a higher CodeBLEU in most cells because dead-code insertion leaves the original AST/dataflow largely intact -- the authors argue this reflects less realistic, more easily flagged perturbations rather than better attack quality. Ablation over 7 configurations (C1-C7) shows removing either the lexical (C2) or structural (C3) channel reduces ASR/Delta_drop, and the full dual-channel model (C7) is best. On the held-out D2A transfer set (OpenSSL/NGINX, unseen during design), HogVul reaches 95.79% (CodeT5), 89.94% (CodeBERT), 91.11% (GraphCodeBERT) ASR without parameter retuning, outperforming ALERT and DIP, which degrade more under distribution shift. Under a fixed-query-budget comparison on Devign (5000/10000/30000 queries), HogVul achieves higher ASR (85.65/85.36/88.18%) than ALERT (62.85/63.33/66.05%) and DIP (58.00/63.65/70.74%) at every budget, and needs far fewer average queries per successful attack (124 vs. 600 for ALERT and 463 for DIP). Against four additional, more recent baselines (ITGen, SLODA, CODA, GraphCodeAttack) on Devign only, HogVul attains the best ASR on GraphCodeBERT (91.60%) and CodeT5 (97.28%) and the best average ASR (92.22%) among the five methods.
core_contribution: HogVul, a black-box adversarial code generation framework against LM-based vulnerability detectors, whose central novelty is a dual-channel PSO-driven optimization that unifies lexical perturbation (variable renaming) and syntax-level perturbation (control-flow restructuring, e.g. for<->while, switch<->if-elseif) in a single search loop. A stagnation counter tracks progress in each channel and triggers a switch to the complementary channel (with the best candidate carried over as a warm start) when a channel stalls, so the two perturbation types cooperate rather than being explored independently as in prior work. Secondary contributions are two new evaluation metrics the authors introduce: ASR per Query (APQ), success rate normalized by query cost, and Code Average Diversity (CAD), mean pairwise normalized Levenshtein distance among substitution vectors in the population.
threat_model: Black-box, query-based access to a target LM-based vulnerability detector M (no gradients/weights). Given vulnerable code c with ground-truth label y=1, the attacker searches for a perturbation delta producing adversarial code c_adv = c + delta such that M misclassifies c_adv as the opposite label (Eq. 1: argmax P(M(c_adv) = 1-y)), subject to preserving program functionality (F(c_adv) = F(c)) and compilability. The paper frames the real-world stake as an increase in false negative rate (FNR) on truly vulnerable code, i.e. adversarially perturbed vulnerabilities evading a detector deployed as a security gate, rather than false positives.
limitations: The authors state future work is needed on 'more reasonable search paradigms and theoretical support,' i.e. the method has no convergence guarantees for the discrete hybrid search space. HogVul's CodeBLEU scores are lower than DIP's in most Table 1 cells, indicating greater semantic/structural drift from the original code -- the paper reframes this as a feature (more realistic, harder-to-detect attacks) rather than addressing it as a naturalness cost. Evaluation is restricted to C/C++ code and three specific pretrained encoder/encoder-decoder detectors (CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, CodeT5); generalization to other languages or detector architectures (e.g. GNN-based like Devign's own model, or LLM-based detectors) is not tested. The syntax-level transformation set (Table 4) is a fixed, hand-curated list of six control-flow rewrite patterns, so coverage of possible structural perturbations is bounded by this catalog. The SLODA and GraphCodeAttack comparisons in Appendix G are partly taken from the original papers' reported numbers (marked with an asterisk) rather than the authors' own reproduction, and SLODA/GraphCodeAttack results are missing for CodeT5 in that table.

datasets_used: Devign (48,000+ C functions from FFmpeg and QEMU) -- main effectiveness eval

DiverseVul (18,945 vulnerable / 330,492 non-vulnerable C/C++ functions across 797 projects, 150+ CWE categories; authors sample 20,000 instances from the first 10 projects, balanced 1:1) -- main effectiveness eval
BigVul (3,754 vulnerabilities from 348 open-source C/C++ projects, 91 vulnerability types) -- main effectiveness eval
D2A (OpenSSL and NGINX subsets selected from the larger 1.3M-example D2A benchmark) -- held-out distribution-shift/transfer eval, not part of the main Table 1 comparison
quantitative_results: ASR 97.28% on Devign/CodeT5 (HogVul), vs. 81.51% ALERT and 53.05% DIP
ASR 89.71% on Devign/CodeBERT (HogVul), vs. 78.35% ALERT and 59.75% DIP
ASR 91.60% on Devign/GraphCodeBERT (HogVul), vs. 79.02% ALERT and 49.45% DIP
Average ASR improvement: +26.37 points over ALERT and +25.72 points over DIP across all dataset/model cells (stated headline: 26.05% average improvement)
Average confidence drop (Delta_drop) improvement: +0.18 over ALERT and +0.07 over DIP on average
D2A transfer ASR: 95.79% (CodeT5), 89.94% (CodeBERT), 91.11% (GraphCodeBERT), all exceeding ALERT and DIP on the same held-out data
Average queries per successful attack on Devign: HogVul 124, ALERT 600, DIP 463
Extended baseline comparison on Devign: HogVul average ASR 92.22% vs. ITGen 84.65%, SLODA 90.31%, GraphCodeAttack 79.26%, CODA 88.27%

Ablation (C1-C7): full model C7 (lexical + structural + semantic-aware init) achieves best ASR (97.28% on CodeT5/Devign); removing lexical perturbation (C2 config) or structural transformation (C3 config) each reduces ASR and Delta_drop
CAD (diversity) consistently higher for HogVul than ALERT/DIP across all reported cells (e.g. 316.45 vs. 147.95 ALERT / 54.66 DIP on Devign/CodeT5)
CodeBLEU: DIP generally highest (e.g. 0.86-0.99 range) vs. HogVul (0.80-0.96) and ALERT (0.29-0.53), reflecting DIP's structure-preserving dead-code-insertion strategy
Control-flow perturbation weight ablation: ASR peaks at lambda=1.8, declining for lambda>=2.5
baselines_compared: ALERT (Yang et al. 2022) -- lexical-only, pretrained-model-guided substitution + greedy/genetic search; primary baseline used throughout Table 1
DIP (Na, Choi, and Lee 2023) -- syntax-only, CodeBERT-localized dead-code insertion; primary baseline used throughout Table 1

ITGen, SLODA, CODA, GraphCodeAttack -- four additional 'more advanced' contemporaneous baselines compared only in Appendix G on the Devign dataset (ITGen and GraphCodeAttack results partly taken from their original papers, marked with an asterisk; SLODA and GraphCodeAttack missing for CodeT5)

MHM, Greedy Search, Genetic Algorithm (GA) -- compared not as attack baselines but as alternative search/optimization algorithms in a t-SNE search-trajectory and population-diversity analysis (Section 5.2), to justify the PSO design choice
